query: does breastfeeding protect against type I diabetes

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: In those at risk, it is generally believed that a virus or other environmental factors trigger the autoimmune beta-cell destruction. Some studies have found an increased development of T1DM in children born to a mother with Coxsackievirus or another Enterovirus during pregnancy. Environmental toxins have also been postulated to play a role. The hygiene hypothesis suggests that improved sanitation relates to the increased development of autoimmune-mediated disorders. It is proposed that reducing childhood exposure to infectious agents leads to the lack of proper immune system development. Dietary factors have also been examined as potential triggers. One study showed an increased development of islet autoimmunity with cow’s milk-protein consumption in study participants with low to moderate-risk HLA-DR genotypes but no significant increased risk in those with a high-risk genotype.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Etiology -- Environmental Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental factors are generally believed to trigger autoimmune β-cell destruction in genetically susceptible people. Some studies have found an increased T1D risk related to infection with Coxsackie virus, enteroviruses, cytomegalovirus, rubella virus, influenza B, mumps virus, and more recently, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19). [9] [10] [11] Other environmental factors that may increase risk include pregnancy and perinatal conditions, childhood vaccination, and dietary factors such as cow's milk and cereal exposure. Research to better understand the exact role of these environmental agents in the etiology of T1D is ongoing.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive data supports that breast milk is significantly superior to formula feeds and animal milk in preventing infections, such as otitis media, lower respiratory tract infections, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) bronchiolitis, sudden infant death syndrome, necrotizing enterocolitis, etc. It also reduces the propensity to develop obesity, allergy, asthma, wheezing, atopy, atopic dermatitis, eczema, celiac disease, Crohn disease, diabetes mellitus type 1, diabetes mellitus type 2, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, acute lymphocytic leukemia, and acute myelogenous leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Breastfeeding -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence shows that breastfeeding lowers the risk of upper respiratory tract infections and diarrhea in newborns. Other postulated benefits include a lower risk of developing asthma, type 1 diabetes, food allergies, and obesity. Breastfeeding may also delay the return of menstruation and offers cost benefits. In the United States, while approximately 75% of neonates are breastfed, numbers decline within a few months of life. Cigarette smoking and alcohol or caffeine use are not valid reasons to stop breastfeeding. Generally, breastfeeding can be stopped between 6 to 12 months of age, although some children are breastfed until they are 2 to 4 years old.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: In T1DM, there is the immune destruction of the beta cells in the pancreatic islets over months or years, causing an absolute deficiency of insulin. Although the exact etiology of T1DM is still unknown, researchers believe there is a genetic predisposition with a strong link with specific HLA (DR and DQ) alleles, especially DRB103-DQB10201 and DRB 10401-DQB10302H. Multiple other genes contribute to heritability, as well. The risk of developing T1DM with no family history is approximately 0.4%, while the offspring of an affected mother is 1% to 4%, the offspring of an affected father is 3% to 8%, and the offspring of both affected parents is as high as 30%. In monozygotic twins, the risk of developing T1DM in the first ten years of diagnosis of the first twin is 30%, with a lifetime risk of around 65%. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Aspart Insulin -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Breastfeeding should be encouraged for all mothers, including those with diabetes, as it is nutritionally and immunologically beneficial for infants. Breastfeeding may also provide long-term metabolic advantages for mother and child, including a reduced risk of type 2 diabetes in mothers with gestational diabetes. However, lactation can increase the risk of overnight hypoglycemia, necessitating insulin dosage adjustments. Exogenous insulin, including insulin analogs like aspart, is excreted into breast milk. Insulin is a natural component of breast milk and may lower the risk of type 1 diabetes in breastfed infants. [14] According to the manufacturer label, breastfeeding benefits and the use of maternal insulin aspart require a thorough risk-benefit analysis, including potential adverse effects on the infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Lactation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to breastfeed or to provide breast milk via expression is a decision that every mother must make. Clinicians must inform our patients about all the benefits that breast milk can provide to their newborn. Breast milk provides ideal nutrition for infants with vitamins, proteins, and fats that are more easily digested than formula. Breast milk contains antibodies from the mother that help babies fight off viruses and bacteria. Other anti-infective factors it provides include immunoglobulin (IgA in particular), white blood cells, whey protein (lysozyme and lactoferrin), and oligosaccharides. It also lowers the baby's risk of asthma, allergies, ear infections, respiratory illnesses, bouts of diarrhea, and the risk of diabetes and obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Function -- Colostrum
retrieved_docs.content: It is the special yellowish milk only secreted in the first 2 to 3 days after the baby's birth, in small amounts (about 30–60 ml/day). It is abundant in white blood cells and antibodies, especially IgA, to fight off pathogens. It contains about 20 specific antibodies against E coli , salmonella, RSV, candida, streptococcus, staphylococcus, cryptosporidium, H pylori , etc. It has more protein, minerals, and fat-soluble vitamins (A, E, and K) than ordinary breast milk. Vitamin A is required to protect the eyes and form epithelium. It contains multiple growth factors. Insulin-like growth factors 1 and 2 are the most commonly found. The epidermal growth colostrum factor also forms the intestinal lining needed to absorb the nutrients. The neonate needs to be fed on colostrum and nothing else now. The composition of colostrum can be altered by maternal conditions such as eclampsia, diabetes, and anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: T1DM is one of the most frequent chronic diseases in children but can start at any age. There has been a steady increase in the incidence and prevalence of T1DM, representing approximately 5% to 10% of people with diabetes. In the United States, there are an estimated 1.24 million people with T1DM, which is expected to grow to 5 million by 2050. Between 2001 and 2009, there was a 21% increase in the prevalence of T1D in those 20 years of age or younger. The most common age of presentation is between the ages of 4 to 6 and early puberty (10 to 14 years). Worldwide, there is also a considerable geographic variation in incidence. The highest reported incidences are in Finland and other Northern European nations, with rates approximately 400 times greater than those seen in China and Venezuela, where there is the lowest reported incidence. In the United States, the highest rate of T1DM is seen in non-Hispanic whites, affecting both females and males almost equally. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The provider will also screen for associated disorders (e.g., thyroid disease, celiac disease, dyslipidemia), ensure screening for complications of chronic hyperglycemia (e.g., retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy), and ongoing healthcare maintenance such as influenza vaccine. A detailed description of diabetes care is documented in the American Diabetes Association Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes publication which is updated each January in the journal Diabetes Care. [2] Strategies should be established to allow the parent to achieve the best glycemic management. Behavior intervention has been found to help with medication compliance and improve outcomes. The parent should be educated about hypoglycemia, the signs, and its management. A recent study revealed that probiotic supplementation before age 3 in infants with type 1 diabetes led to a reduction in the development of pancreatic islet cell autoimmunity.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Both genetic and environmental contributions lead to immune-mediated loss of beta cell function resulting in hyperglycemia and life-long insulin dependence. In an individual at risk (human leukocyte antigen (HLA) haplotype accounts for 30% to 50% of their genetic risk. More than 50 other genes have been found through candidate gene and genome-wide association studies.  A "triggering" insult (e.g., maternal and intrauterine environment, exposure to viruses, host microbiome, diet and many other factors are thought to contribute to disease susceptibility) is suspected to initiate a process that recruits antigen-presenting cells to transport beta cell self-antigens to autoreactive T cells. Through failures of self-tolerance, these T cells mediate beta-cell killing and inflammation leading to insulinopenia and symptomatic diabetes. Recently, preclinical stages of type 1 diabetes have been recognized. Stage 1 is defined by the presence of beta cell autoimmunity, but normal glucose-handling, stage 2 is defined by abnormal glucose handling but no overt symptoms, and finally stage 3 is defined by clinically-apparent symptoms of insulinopenia. Progression through these stages may take years. Although the pre-clinical staging is not usually clinically relevant, research focusing on interventions in the pre-clinical groups may prove to delay or prevent the onset of type 1 diabetes. [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Educate about harms of smoking

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to note that insulin requirements vary across the lifespan and under specific circumstances. For example, larger insulin doses are normally required during puberty, pregnancy, when steroids are given, and with the development of obesity. Individuals need less insulin when they are engaged in aerobic exercise and during the “honeymoon period.” The honeymoon period occurs soon after diagnosis when there is a temporary recovery of beta-cell function.

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Administration -- Special Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding patients : GLP-1 agonists are a class of medications used to manage type 2 diabetes. Limited data on the excretion of GLP-1 agonists in human breast milk is available. Therefore, caution is recommended when considering GLP-1RA during lactation.

retrieved_docs.title: Infant Nutrition Requirements and Options -- Issues of Concern -- Breastfeeding
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding is now much more than a medical issue- it transcends social, economic, cultural, and geographic realms. AAP recommends exclusive breastfeeding up to six months of age. [29] Also, continued breastfeeding is recommended, as other foods are introduced. Breastfeeding promotes mother-infant bonding and is known to improve maternal and child health. [29] Breastfeeding has also been shown to reduce upper respiratory tract infections, middle ear infections (23%), lower respiratory tract infections (72%), and diarrhea in newborns. It is also suggested to reduce the risk of asthma (up to 40% reduction), food allergies, SIDS (30%), inflammatory bowel disease (40%), type 2 diabetes (40%), and obesity. [29] On the mother's side, breastfeeding has been shown to delay the return of menstruation, and due to increased metabolic demands can help with faster return to pre-pregnancy weight. Besides, a reduction in breast cancer has been shown with a longer duration of total breastfeeding for more than 12 months. [29] If 90% of women in the United States breastfeed for up to 6 months, one estimate predicts potential healthcare savings of up to 13 billion dollars. [30] As part of a global push to improve breastfeeding rate, WHO and UNICEF implemented the baby-friendly hospital initiative (BFHI) in 1991 and has since been revised [31] , which has now been widely adopted by multiple hospitals across the world. BFHI framework uses the ten steps to successful breastfeeding, which are intended to promote breastfeeding. [29] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The provider will also screen for associated disorders (e.g., thyroid disease, celiac disease, dyslipidemia), ensure screening for complications of chronic hyperglycemia (e.g., retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy), and ongoing healthcare maintenance such as influenza vaccine. A detailed description of diabetes care is documented in the American Diabetes Association Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes publication which is updated each January in the journal Diabetes Care. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 5 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is associated with the pathophysiology of داء السكري أثناء الحمل (gestational diabetes), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and رضح الولادة (post‑partum lactation). In each of these conditions, the underlying pathophysiological mechanisms are described using the same qualifying concept, indicating a shared thematic element across a metabolic disorder of pregnancy, an oral motor disorder, and the physiological changes of lactation after birth. This common qualifier helps clinicians recognize related mechanistic pathways when evaluating and managing these diverse yet pathophysiologically linked conditions.

In addition, مرض السكري has rizikové faktory, highlighting the presence of specific risk factors that contribute to the development and progression of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: by aligning surgical, nutritional, epidemiologic, pharmacologic, pathophysiologic and preventive interventions across diverse obstetric, neonatal, cystic, cardiovascular, metabolic and infectious conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms of diabetes that map directly to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also a manifestation of مرض السكري, representing gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance within the broader diabetes spectrum. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this emergency contraceptive should be avoided in individuals with diabetes. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy in diabetic patients. Finally, malfunction of the human INSR protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of مرض السكري, underscoring the role of insulin‑receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, contraindications, complications, and molecular mechanisms associated with diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2433
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is linked clinically to pancreatic disorders that are not themselves diabetes and to retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring of both pancreatic and ocular health in affected individuals. Hormonal agents such as desogestrel, ethynodioldiacetát, and norgestrel are contraindicated in patients with diabetes, indicating that these medications should be avoided or used with caution in this population. Additionally, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” is recognized as a specific manifestation of diabetes, highlighting the particular risks and management considerations for diabetic women during the perinatal period. Together, these relationships emphasize the importance of tailored therapeutic choices and comprehensive complication surveillance in the care of people with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1699
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms or presentations that map to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes). The hormonal contraceptive desogestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that use of this medication should be avoided in diabetic individuals. Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a distinct clinical scenario that falls under مرض السكري, highlighting the importance of careful management of glucose levels during the perinatal period. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Additionally, the term rizikové faktory (risk factors) is linked to مرض السكري, underscoring that various risk factors contribute to the development and progression of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse manifestations, comorbidities, contraindications, and risk considerations associated with مرض السكري.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2690
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic nephropathy (اعتلال الكلية السكري), adult respiratory failure/insufficiency/arrest (respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult)), cystitis (بيلة سِّيسْتينِية), fibrotic disease (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), postpartum hemorrhage (رضح الولادة), glandular tumor (عضال غدي), and paralysis (فالج) are all conditions for which mortality has been identified as a significant outcome. The same conditions are also recognized as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that appropriate preventive strategies and management can potentially reduce the risk of death associated with each disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) includes specific forms such as DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), which is mapped to the broader category of diabetes, and the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is an inverse subclass of diabetes. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is a central medical condition that connects a broad spectrum of disorders, treatments, and complications. Various neonatal forms of diabetes—including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are mapped to diabetes mellitus, indicating that these early‑life presentations are considered manifestations of the disease. Diabetes mellitus also underlies glucose intolerance that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, as reflected by an inverse‑isa relationship with “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium.” Certain drugs, such as the progestin desogestrel and the agent ethynodioldiacetát, are contraindicated in patients with diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Clinically, diabetes mellitus is associated with several serious complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 671
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that connects a wide array of metabolic, developmental, obstetric, ophthalmic, musculoskeletal, infectious and pharmacologic concepts. All recognized forms of neonatal diabetes—including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal 1, and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are mapped to مرض السكري, underscoring its relevance in early‑life glucose dysregulation. In the obstetric domain, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that complicates gestation, childbirth or the puerperium is classified as an inverse subclass of مرض السكري, highlighting maternal‑fetal risk. Norgestrel is explicitly contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, reflecting an important drug‑disease interaction for contraceptive planning. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is recorded as a child of مرض السكري, and both entities share the comorbidity label
